1609	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict along a basic-open immersion]
1610	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1611	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1612	  % LEAN STATUS: seam-1 sub-piece, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
1613	  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict (TODO). Mathlib provides the
1614	  % SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType local-generators datum (GeneratingSections /
1615	  % LocalGeneratorsData) but no restriction-of-generators lemma along an open
1616	  % immersion; this is the project-built piece.
1617	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1618	  modules. Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) carries a
1619	  finite generating family of sections, i.e. a datum exhibiting \(\F|_Y\) as globally
1620	  generated by finitely many sections (a finite \(\mathrm{LocalGeneratorsData}\) /
1621	  \(\mathrm{GeneratingSections}\) for \(\F.\mathrm{over}\,Y\)). Then for any open immersion
1622	  \(D(g) \hookrightarrow Y\) the restricted family — the images of those generators under
1623	  the section-restriction \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) — is again a finite
1624	  generating family of \(\F|_{D(g)}\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open
1625	  (a family that generates every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(D(g)\)),
1626	  and the index set, and hence finiteness, is unchanged.
1627	\end{lemma}
1628	
1629	\begin{proof}
1630	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1631	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1632	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the open immersion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow Y\) the
1633	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_{D(g)}\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in D(g)\) the stalk
1634	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1635	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1636	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I} \to \F|_{D(g)}\) is surjective on every stalk over \(D(g)\), so
1637	  \(\{s_i|_{D(g)}\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_{D(g)}\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1638	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved.
1639	\end{proof}

1641	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1642	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1643	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1644	  % LEAN STATUS: seam-1 sub-piece, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
1645	  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover (TODO). Mathlib supplies
1646	  % PrimeSpectrum.isBasis_basic_opens and quasi-compactness of an affine scheme, but
1647	  % the packaged refinement-to-finite-standard-subcover (output t with Ideal.span t = ⊤)
1648	  % is the project-built assembly.
1649	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1650	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1651	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1652	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1653	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1654	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1655	\end{lemma}
1656	
1657	\begin{proof}
1658	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1659	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1660	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1661	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1662	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1663	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1664	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1665	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1666	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1667	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1668	\end{proof}

1670	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1671	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1673	  % LEAN STATUS: seam-1 sub-piece, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
1674	  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi (TODO). The equivalence between a
1675	  % finite generating family and a finite-free epimorphism of O-modules is the
1676	  % project-built repackaging of the generators datum as an epi out of O^{⊕I}.
1677	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules. Then
1678	  \(\F|_Y\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1679	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1680	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\).
1681	\end{lemma}
1682	
1683	\begin{proof}
1684	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1685	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1686	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1687	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1688	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1689	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1690	  finite generating family.
1691	\end{proof}

1780	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of

1786	\begin{proof}

1786	\begin{proof}
1787	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1788	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1789	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1790	  affine).}
1791	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1792	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1793	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1794	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1795	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1796	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1797	
1798	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1799	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1800	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1801	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1802	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1803	  \[
1804	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1805	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1806	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1807	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1808	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1809	  \]
1810	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1811	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1812	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1813	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1814	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1815	
1816	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1817	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1818	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1819	\end{proof}

1974	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1975	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1976	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1977	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1978	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1979	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every

1984	\begin{proof}
1985	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1986	  Throughout, flatness is established by the standard locality criteria for module flatness
1987	  over a commutative ring, applied on the \emph{base} ring; the source cover \(\{W_j\}\)
1988	  enters only through the stalks of \(\F\), and is never treated as a covering family of any
1989	  base ring.
1990	
1991	  \emph{Step 1 — free \(\Rightarrow\) flat on each patch.} Each \((M_j)_f\) is free over
1992	  \(A_f\) by hypothesis, and a free module is flat. Hence each \((M_j)_f\) is flat over
1993	  \(A_f\).
1994	
1995	  \emph{Step 2 — pointwise flatness of \(\F\) over the base on \(p^{-1}(V)\).} Fix a point
1996	  \(x \in p^{-1}(V) = X_V\) and put \(s = p(x) \in V = D(f)\). The cover \(\{W_j\}\) of
1997	  \(p^{-1}(U_0) \supseteq p^{-1}(V)\) contains a patch \(W_j \ni x\). On \(W_j\),
1998	  quasi-coherence identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of
1999	  the finite \(B_j\)-module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) (finite by
2000	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}); consequently the sections of \(\F\) over a
2001	  basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W_j\) are the localization \((M_j)_g\)
2002	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}), and passing to the limit the stalk
2003	  \(\F_x\) is the localization of \(M_j\) at the prime of \(B_j\) corresponding to \(x\).
2004	  Because \(s \in D(f)\), the element \(f\) is invertible in the local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) and
2005	  hence acts invertibly on \(\F_x\); thus \(\F_x\) is equally a localization of the inverted
2006	  module \((M_j)_f\). The local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) is the localization of \(A_f\) at the prime
2007	  of \(s\) (a point of \(D(f) = \Spec A_f\)), and localization preserves flatness; since
2008	  \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\), the localized module \(\F_x\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,s}\).
2009	  Therefore \(\F\) is flat over the base at every point of \(p^{-1}(V)\): the stalk \(\F_x\)
2010	  is a flat \(\OO_{S,p(x)}\)-module for all \(x \in p^{-1}(V)\).
2011	
2012	  \emph{Step 3 — from pointwise flatness to \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).}
2013	  Let \(U \le V\) and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) be affine, and write \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\),
2014	  \(B = \Gamma(X, W)\), and \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\); here \(N\) is a \(B\)-module and \(B\) an
2015	  \(R\)-algebra. We prove \(N\) flat over \(R\) by reducing first on the base, then on the
2016	  source.
2017	
2018	  \emph{Base reduction.} By the base-maximal locality criterion for flatness it suffices to
2019	  show, for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\), that the localization
2020	  \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R\); equivalently, that \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over
2021	  the local ring \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\), where \(x \in U \subseteq V = D(f)\) is the
2022	  point corresponding to \(\mathfrak p\). In particular \(\OO_{S,x}\) is a localization of
2023	  \(A_f\).
2024	
2025	  \emph{Source reduction.} Flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over the local base
2026	  \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\) is checked at the points of the source: it suffices that
2027	  for every point \(y \in W\) lying over a generization of \(x\) the stalk \(\F_y\) — the
2028	  localization of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) at the prime of \(B \otimes_R R_{\mathfrak p}\)
2029	  corresponding to \(y\) — is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\). Each such \(y\) lies
2030	  in \(p^{-1}(V)\), so by Step 2 \(\F_y\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\). As \(p(y)\) is a
2031	  generization of \(x\), the comparison \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,p(y)}\) is a localization,
2032	  hence flat, and transitivity of flatness upgrades ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\)''
2033	  to ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,x}\)''. The passage from these stalk-flatness statements
2034	  to flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) records that module flatness
2035	  over a local base ring is detected at the points of the source affine; this source-side
2036	  locality is the project-built geometric glue, no single Mathlib lemma localizing a module
2037	  at the maximal ideals of an algebra over the base being available.
2038	
2039	  Combining the two reductions, \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) for
2040	  every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\); hence \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over
2041	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
2042	\end{proof}

810	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
811	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
812	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
813	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
814	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
815	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
816	\end{lemma}

1402	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1403	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1404	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1405	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules. Then
1406	  \(\F|_Y\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1407	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1408	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\).
1409	\end{lemma}
1410	
1411	\begin{proof}
1412	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1413	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1414	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1415	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1416	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1417	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1418	  finite generating family.
1419	\end{proof}

1443	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules of finite type,
1444	  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
1445	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for

1454	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}

1457	  member of which \(\F\) carries a finite generating family of sections. The intersections
1458	  \(\{Y_\alpha \cap W\}\) form an open cover of the affine \(W\). By the finite standard
1459	  subcover refinement \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} there is a finite family

1462	  \(Y_\alpha \cap W\) — in particular \(D(g_i) \subseteq W\) is affine, and \(D(g_i)\)
1463	  embeds by an open immersion into the member \(Y_\alpha\) on which \(\F\) is finitely
1464	  generated. By the restriction-of-generators lemma

1466	  restricts to a finite generating family of \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\), so \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\) is

1476	\begin{lemma}[Affine $\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma$ adjunction with its $\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ counit]
1477	  \label{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}

1479	  \mathlibok
1480	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}).}
1481	  On the affine scheme \(\Spec R\) the associated-sheaf functor
1482	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules}\) is left adjoint to the
1483	  affine section functor \(\Gamma : (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{Mod}\,R\) (Mathlib's
1484	  \(\mathrm{moduleSpecΓFunctor}\), evaluation of a sheaf of modules at the top open). The counit of
1485	  this adjunction is the natural transformation
1486	  \(\Gamma(-)\,\widetilde{\phantom{x}} \to \mathrm{id}\) whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\)
1487	  is \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \top)} \to \mathcal{M}\), and
1488	  the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful.

1492	[Half 2, seam 2 — $\Gamma$ on affine quasi-coherent modules sends epis to surjections]
1493	  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1494	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1495	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1496	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}

1499	  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$

1500	  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
1501	  % epi over an affine.
1502	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1503	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1504	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1505	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1506	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1507	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1508	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1509	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1510	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1511	  \(B\)-module map.
1512	\end{lemma}
1513	
1514	\begin{proof}
1515	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1516	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1517	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1518	  affine).}
1519	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1520	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1521	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1522	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1523	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1524	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1525	
1526	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1527	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1528	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1529	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1530	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1531	  \[
1532	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1533	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1534	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1535	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1536	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1537	  \]
1538	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1539	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1540	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1541	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1542	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1543	
1544	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1545	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1546	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1547	\end{proof}

1587	  \(B\)-module.

1608	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (free generation
1609	  over an affine).}
1610	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), write \(R = \Gamma(X, V)\) with
1611	  \(V \cong \Spec R\), and let \(\F\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules. Suppose there
1612	  is a finite \(R\)-module \(N\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_V\)-modules
1613	  \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F\) out of the associated sheaf of \(N\) (the case
1614	  \(N = R^{\oplus I}\), \(I\) finite, being the free-generation datum
1615	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\)). Then \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite
1616	  \(R\)-module.
1617	\end{lemma}

1619	\begin{proof}

1620	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}
1621	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB.}
1622	  This is the free-source specialisation of
1623	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}; what remains is to discharge that
1624	  lemma's two source-side hypotheses for \(\mathcal{G} = \widetilde{N}\). By the affine
1625	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma\) adjunction \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the unit
1626	  at \(N\) is an isomorphism \(N \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\widetilde{N}, V)\) (the left
1627	  adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is fully faithful), so \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, V)\) is a
1628	  finite \(R\)-module because \(N\) is; and the counit component at \(\widetilde{N}\),
1629	  namely \(\widetilde{N}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\), is an isomorphism (a fully-faithful left
1630	  adjoint has invertible counit on its image), supplying the source quasi-coherence

1633	  \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F\) yields that \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite

1638	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}

1641	        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,

1656	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1657	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1658	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1659	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1660	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1661	\end{lemma}
1662	
1663	\begin{proof}
1664	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
1665	        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
1666	        lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi}
1667	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1668	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1669	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1670	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1671	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\). Cover extraction
1672	  \cref{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated} gives a finite
1673	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) spanning the unit ideal, with each \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\))
1674	  affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) globally generated by finitely many sections, equivalently a
1675	  finite-free epimorphism \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\). The
1676	  free-epi base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi} applied on each affine \(D(g)\)
1677	  makes \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). The locality reduction
1678	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover} then glues these per-member

1682	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1683	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1684	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1685	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1686	  % LEAN STATUS: G3 — project-built. Flatness of Γ(F,W) over Γ(S,U) is obtained by the
1687	  % BASE-ring locality criteria — NO source patch W_j is ever treated as a covering
1688	  % family of a base ring (that was a prior error). Verified Mathlib anchors
1689	  % (Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Localization / .Stability / .Basic):
1690	  %   Module.flat_of_localized_maximal   [verified] — reduce to maximal ideals 𝔭 of R=Γ(S,U)
1691	  %   Module.flat_iff_of_isLocalization  [verified] — pass to R_𝔭 = O_{S,x}, x ∈ D(f)
1692	  %                                                    (so O_{S,x} is a localization of A_f)
1693	  %   Module.Flat.of_free                [verified] — free (M_j)_f ⟹ flat over A_f
1694	  %   IsLocalization.flat + Module.Flat.trans [verified] — localization preserves flatness
1695	  % The stalk = localization-of-patch-module identification is quasi-coherence
1696	  % (lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, gap2 DONE). The remaining source-side glue
1697	  % — flatness over a LOCAL base checked at the points of the source affine — has NO
1698	  % single Mathlib lemma (the *_of_localized_maximal family localizes at maximals of the
1699	  % BASE or of a base-localization, not at maximals of an R-algebra B=Γ(X,W)); it is the
1700	  % project-built piece. Built after G1.
1701	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1702	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1703	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1704	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1705	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1706	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1707	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1708	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1709	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1710	\end{lemma}
1711	
1712	\begin{proof}
1713	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1714	  Throughout, flatness is established by the standard locality criteria for module flatness
1715	  over a commutative ring, applied on the \emph{base} ring; the source cover \(\{W_j\}\)
1716	  enters only through the stalks of \(\F\), and is never treated as a covering family of any
1717	  base ring.
1718	
1719	  \emph{Step 1 — free \(\Rightarrow\) flat on each patch.} Each \((M_j)_f\) is free over
1720	  \(A_f\) by hypothesis, and a free module is flat. Hence each \((M_j)_f\) is flat over
1721	  \(A_f\).
1722	
1723	  \emph{Step 2 — pointwise flatness of \(\F\) over the base on \(p^{-1}(V)\).} Fix a point
1724	  \(x \in p^{-1}(V) = X_V\) and put \(s = p(x) \in V = D(f)\). The cover \(\{W_j\}\) of
1725	  \(p^{-1}(U_0) \supseteq p^{-1}(V)\) contains a patch \(W_j \ni x\). On \(W_j\),
1726	  quasi-coherence identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of
1727	  the finite \(B_j\)-module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) (finite by
1728	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}); consequently the sections of \(\F\) over a
1729	  basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W_j\) are the localization \((M_j)_g\)
1730	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}), and passing to the limit the stalk
1731	  \(\F_x\) is the localization of \(M_j\) at the prime of \(B_j\) corresponding to \(x\).
1732	  Because \(s \in D(f)\), the element \(f\) is invertible in the local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) and
1733	  hence acts invertibly on \(\F_x\); thus \(\F_x\) is equally a localization of the inverted
1734	  module \((M_j)_f\). The local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) is the localization of \(A_f\) at the prime
1735	  of \(s\) (a point of \(D(f) = \Spec A_f\)), and localization preserves flatness; since
1736	  \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\), the localized module \(\F_x\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,s}\).
1737	  Therefore \(\F\) is flat over the base at every point of \(p^{-1}(V)\): the stalk \(\F_x\)
1738	  is a flat \(\OO_{S,p(x)}\)-module for all \(x \in p^{-1}(V)\).
1739

1740	  \emph{Step 3 — from pointwise flatness to \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).}
1741	  Let \(U \le V\) and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) be affine, and write \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\),
1742	  \(B = \Gamma(X, W)\), and \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\); here \(N\) is a \(B\)-module and \(B\) an
1743	  \(R\)-algebra. We prove \(N\) flat over \(R\) by reducing first on the base, then on the
1744	  source.
1745	
1746	  \emph{Base reduction.} By the base-maximal locality criterion for flatness it suffices to
1747	  show, for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\), that the localization
1748	  \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R\); equivalently, that \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over
1749	  the local ring \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\), where \(x \in U \subseteq V = D(f)\) is the
1750	  point corresponding to \(\mathfrak p\). In particular \(\OO_{S,x}\) is a localization of
1751	  \(A_f\).
1752	
1753	  \emph{Source reduction.} Flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over the local base
1754	  \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\) is checked at the points of the source: it suffices that
1755	  for every point \(y \in W\) lying over a generization of \(x\) the stalk \(\F_y\) — the
1756	  localization of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) at the prime of \(B \otimes_R R_{\mathfrak p}\)
1757	  corresponding to \(y\) — is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\). Each such \(y\) lies
1758	  in \(p^{-1}(V)\), so by Step 2 \(\F_y\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\). As \(p(y)\) is a
1759	  generization of \(x\), the comparison \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,p(y)}\) is a localization,
1760	  hence flat, and transitivity of flatness upgrades ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\)''
1761	  to ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,x}\)''. The passage from these stalk-flatness statements
1762	  to flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) records that module flatness
1763	  over a local base ring is detected at the points of the source affine; this source-side
1764	  locality is the project-built geometric glue, no single Mathlib lemma localizing a module
1765	  at the maximal ideals of an algebra over the base being available.
1766	
1767	  Combining the two reductions, \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) for
1768	  every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\); hence \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over
1769	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1770	\end{proof}
1771	
1772	
1773	\begin{theorem}
1774	
1775

1779	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1780	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1781	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1782	  % NOTE (re-signed): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
1783	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
1784	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any

1786	  % the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is the source of truth for the exact
1787	  % binders and the full flatness conclusion.

1789	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a

1800	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
1801	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1802	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1803	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1804	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1805	  %

1807	  % entail QuasiCompact; without it the statement is false (counterexample:
1808	  % ⊔_i Spec ℤ → Spec ℤ with a finite-type torsion sheaf). It is the extra clause
1809	  % promoting `LocallyOfFiniteType` to `FiniteType`, and there is no instance
1810	  % `LocallyOfFiniteType → QuasiCompact`. The header above carries it, and the Step-1
1811	  % proof prose below invokes it directly (no false "finite type ⟹ quasi-compact" step).
1812	
1813	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1814	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1815	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1816	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1817	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1818	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1819	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1820	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1821	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1822	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1823	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1824	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1825	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1826	
1827	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1828	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1829	\end{theorem}
1830	
1831	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1832	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1833	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1834	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1835	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1836	% by restricting to a non-empty
1837	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1838	\begin{proof}
1839	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1840	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1841	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1842	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness

14	# Blueprint Clean Subagent
15	
16	Your job is to read and refine a blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) to ensure it meets strict quality, purity, and citation standards.
17	
18	## Your Tasks
19	
20	1. **Strip Lean Leakage & Project History:**
21	   - Remove any mention of Lean tactics, typeclass notes, or specific Lean implementation strategies.
22	   - Remove project history references like "since iteration N", "our failed route", etc.
23	   - Ensure the blueprint reads as a timeless, standalone mathematical document.
24	2. **Trim Verbosity:**
25	   - Remove useless, overly conversational paragraphs.
26	   - Remove passages that are irrelevant or redundantly discuss things already implemented, unless they serve a mathematical purpose.
27	   - Keep the prose concise and mathematically precise.
28	3. **Enforce Citation Discipline & Add Quotes:**
29	   - Verify every block derived from a reference has a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`.
30	   - **If the quote is missing** but the reference exists in `references/`, you MUST open the actual reference `.pdf` or `.tex` file (using the `.md` index file as a pointer to find the section/page), extract the verbatim quote, and add it to the blueprint.
31	   - **If a reference SHOULD be cited** but isn't mentioned by the writer, and the source is missing from `references/`, spawn a `reference-retriever` to fetch it.
32	4. **Fix LaTeX:**
33	   - Correct basic LaTeX syntax errors.
34	   - Ensure `\uses{}` and `\label{}` cross-references are formatted correctly.
35	
36	## Spawning a Retriever
37	If you identify a missing reference, spawn it using:
38	```bash
39	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name reference-retriever --slug <slug> --directive-file <file> --write-domain 'references/**'
40	```
41	
42	Return your outcome and the path to your report.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
