1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
5	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
6	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
7	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
8	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
9	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
10	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
11	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
12	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
13	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
14	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
15	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
16	of the trivial sheaf.
17	
18	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
19	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
20	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
21	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
22	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
23	
24	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
25	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
26	
27	\begin{definition}
28	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
29	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
30	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
31	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix}
32	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
33	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
34	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
35	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
36	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
37	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
38	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
39	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
40	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
43	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
44	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
45	  \[
46	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
47	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
48	  \]
49	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
50	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
51	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
52	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
53	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
54	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
55	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
56	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
57	\end{definition}
58	
59	\begin{definition}
60	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
61	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
62	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
63	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
64	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme}
65	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
66	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
67	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
68	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
69	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
70	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
71	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
72	  %  by putting
73	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
74	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
75	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
76	  \[
77	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
78	  \]
79	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
80	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
81	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
82	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
83	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
84	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
85	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
86	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
87	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
88	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
89	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
90	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	\begin{definition}
94	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
95	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
96	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
97	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
98	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle}
99	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
100	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
101	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
102	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
103	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
104	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
105	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
106	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
107	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
108	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
109	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
110	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
111	  \[
112	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
113	  \]
114	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
115	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
116	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
117	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
118	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
119	  \[
120	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
121	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
122	  \]
123	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
124	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
125	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
126	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
127	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
128	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\section{The functor of points}
132	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
133	
134	\begin{definition}
135	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
136	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
138	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
139	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
140	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
141	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
142	  % "represents the
143	  %  contravariant functor
144	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
145	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
146	  %  set of all equivalence classes
147	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
148	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
149	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
150	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
151	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
152	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
153	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
154	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
155	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
156	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
157	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
158	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
159	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
160	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
161	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
162	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
163	  \[
164	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
165	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
166	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
167	  \]
168	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
169	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
170	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
171	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
172	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
173	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
174	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
175	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
176	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{The universal property}
180	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
181	
182	\begin{theorem}
183	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
184	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
185	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
186	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
187	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
188	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
189	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
190	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
191	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
192	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
193	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
194	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
195	  %  contravariant functor
196	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
197	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
198	  %  set of all equivalence classes
199	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
200	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
201	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
202	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
203	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
204	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
205	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
206	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
207	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
208	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
209	  \[
210	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
211	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
212	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
213	    \qquad
214	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
215	      \;\longmapsto\;
216	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
217	  \]
218	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
219	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
220	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
221	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
222	  universal family.
223	\end{theorem}
224	
225	\begin{proof}
226	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
227	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
228	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
229	    def:grassmannian_functor}
230	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
231	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
232	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
233	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
234	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
235	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
236	
237	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
238	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
239	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
240	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
241	  the composite
242	  \[
243	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
244	      \xhookrightarrow{\ \iota_I\ } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
245	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
246	  \]
247	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
248	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
249	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
250	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
251	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
252	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
253	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
254	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
255	
256	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
257	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
258	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
259	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
260	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
261	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
262	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
263	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
264	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
265	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
266	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
267	
268	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
269	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
270	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
271	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
272	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
273	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
274	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
275	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
276	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
277	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
278	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
279	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
280	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
281	
282	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
283	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
284	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
285	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
286	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
287	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
288	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
289	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
290	
291	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
292	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
293	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
294	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
295	\end{proof}
296

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	% This chapter blueprints the Mathlib-absent infrastructure that the section
5	% graded ring R(X_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,L_s^{⊗m}) and section graded module
6	% M(X_s,F_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,F_s⊗L_s^{⊗m}) of Picard_QuotScheme.tex
7	% (def:sectionGradedRing / def:sectionGradedModule) depend on. Those two
8	% definitions are NOT restated here; this chapter supplies the three separable
9	% infrastructure layers they \uses{}. The target Lean file is
10	% AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SectionGradedRing.lean (1:1 slug, to be scaffolded).
11	
12	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
13	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
14	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
15	
16	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
17	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
18	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
19	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
20	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
21	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
22	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
23	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
24	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
25	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
26	
27	\begin{enumerate}
28	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
29	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
30	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
31	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
32	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
33	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
34	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
35	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
36	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
37	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
38	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
39	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
40	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
41	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
42	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
43	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
44	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
45	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
46	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
47	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
48	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
49	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
50	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
51	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
52	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
53	    \emph{module} structure on
54	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
55	\end{enumerate}
56	
57	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
58	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
59	
60	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
61	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
62	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
63	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
64	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
65	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
66	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
67	monoidal structure applies.
68	
69	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
70	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
71	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
72	  \mathlibok
73	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
74	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
75	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
76	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
77	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
78	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
79	  and an object \(U\),
80	  \[
81	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
82	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
83	  \]
84	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
85	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
86	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
87	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
88	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
89	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
93	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
94	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
97	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
98	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
99	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
100	  \[
101	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
102	  \]
103	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
104	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
105	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
106	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
107	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
108	\end{lemma}
109	
110	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
111	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
112	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
113	  \mathlibok
114	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
115	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
116	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
117	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
118	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
119	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
120	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
121	  the section graded ring.
122	\end{lemma}
123	
124	\begin{definition}\leanok
125	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
126	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
127	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
128	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
129	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
130	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
131	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
132	  % L2282-L2297).
133	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
134	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
135	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
136	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
137	  %  $$
138	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
139	  %  $$
140	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
141	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
142	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
143	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
144	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
145	  %  $$
146	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
147	  %  =
148	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
149	  %  $$"
150	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
151	  product).}
152	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
153	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
154	  \[
155	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
156	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
157	  \]
158	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
159	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
160	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
161	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
162	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
163	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
164	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
165	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
166	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
167	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
168	\end{definition}
169	
170	\begin{definition}\leanok
171	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
172	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
174	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
175	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
176	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
177	  % L4220-L4228).
178	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
179	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
180	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
181	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
182	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
183	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
184	  %  exactly $n$ times."
185	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
186	  powers).}
187	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
188	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
189	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
190	  on \(m\):
191	  \[
192	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
193	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
194	    \qquad
195	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
196	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
197	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
198	  \]
199	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
200	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
201	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
202	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
203	  invertible.
204	\end{definition}
205	
206	\begin{definition}\leanok
207	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
208	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
209	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
210	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
211	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
212	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
213	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
214	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
215	  \[
216	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
217	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
218	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
219	  \]
220	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
221	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
222	  \mathcal{F}\).
223	\end{definition}
224	
225	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
226	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
228	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
229	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
230	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
231	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
232	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
233	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
234	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
235	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
236	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
237	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
238	  %  (formulation omitted)."
239	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
240	  power isomorphisms).}
241	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
242	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \mu_{m,m'} :
245	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
246	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
247	  \]
248	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
249	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
250	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
251	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
252	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj,
257	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
258	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
259	  We do \emph{not} appeal to a monoidal structure on the whole category
260	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is available --- but only to the comparison
261	  on tensor \emph{powers} of the single invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\). On such
262	  locally free factors the construction is elementary, and right-exactness of the
263	  tensor product is never invoked.
264	
265	  \textbf{A local-iso criterion.} A morphism of sheaves of
266	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is an isomorphism as soon as it is one locally, i.e.\
267	  after restriction to each member of an open cover of \(X\): two-sided inverses
268	  defined over the members of a cover and agreeing on overlaps glue to a global
269	  inverse. We invoke this criterion \emph{only} on tensor products of sheaves
270	  that are locally free of rank one. On a trivialising open \(U\) where
271	  \(\mathcal{L}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), every tensor power restricts to
272	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes k}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) as well; the tensor product
273	  presheaf there takes the value \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
274	  \mathcal{O}_X(U) = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\); and the sheafification unit \(\eta\)
275	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) restricts over \(U\) to the
276	  identity. Hence over each such \(U\) the structural comparison maps used below
277	  --- the unitor, associator, and braiding of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
278	  descended objectwise from \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} --- are
279	  visibly bijective, and the criterion promotes them to genuine isomorphisms in
280	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). The subtlety that obstructs a general monoidal
281	  descent --- that the sheafification comparison
282	  \(\eta_{\mathcal P} \otimes \mathrm{id}\) need not be locally injective when
283	  \(\mathcal P\) is arbitrary, the tensor product being only right exact --- does
284	  not bite here: that object is never among the ones we test. The only maps we
285	  test are comparisons between locally free factors, where the unit is locally an
286	  identity and the comparison is a genuine local isomorphism.
287	
288	  Concretely, each structural map is obtained as the image under the
289	  sheafification functor \(\mathrm{sheafification} : \PShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
290	  \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of
291	  the corresponding natural transformation supplied by the presheaf-level
292	  monoidal structure \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}: the associator
293	  \(\alpha\), the left/right unitors \(\lambda,\rho\), and the braiding
294	  \(\beta\) of \(\PShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). Sheafification is a functor, hence
295	  carries each such presheaf-level natural transformation to a morphism of
296	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\); these are the maps the local-isomorphism criterion
297	  above promotes to isomorphisms on locally free factors.
298	
299	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
300	  \(m = 0\),
301	  \[
302	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
303	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
304	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
305	  \]
306	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, an isomorphism by the
307	  criterion. For the inductive step the recursive definition
308	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
309	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
310	  \[
311	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
312	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
313	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
314	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
315	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
316	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
317	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
318	  \]
319	  using first the associator and then the braiding of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
320	  each an isomorphism by the criterion. A second associator and the inductive
321	  comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) then identify the
322	  right-hand side with
323	  \[
324	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
325	      \mathcal{L}
326	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \otimes \mathrm{id}\ }
327	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
328	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m' + 1)},
329	  \]
330	  the final equality being once more the recursive definition. The composite is
331	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
332	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
333	
334	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} Every map appearing in
335	  the construction is a structural comparison isomorphism between locally free
336	  factors, so the required coherences are equalities of morphisms that may be
337	  checked locally on a common trivialising cover. Over such an open every sheaf
338	  in sight becomes \(\mathcal{O}_U\) and every comparison map becomes the
339	  identity, whence both sides of the commutativity square (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus
340	  \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and of the associativity
341	  diagram (the two bracketings of
342	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
343	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) reduce to the
344	  same local identity. Gluing the local equalities yields the global cocycle
345	  relations: the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\) is coherent in the
346	  pentagon/hexagon sense.
347	
348	  \textbf{Remark on the principled route.} One could instead obtain \(\mu\) from
349	  a genuine monoidal structure on \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) descended from the
350	  presheaf-level monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib})
351	  along sheafification, via the localized-monoidal / reflective-localization
352	  template for sheaf categories. That principled construction requires a
353	  monoidal-closed structure on \(\PshMod\) that is not built in this chapter, and
354	  is deferred. The bespoke line-bundle construction above is all the section
355	  graded ring requires.
356	\end{proof}
357	
358	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
359	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
360	
361	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
362	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
363	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
364	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
365	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
366	global sections.
367	
368	\begin{definition}[Section multiplication]
369	  \label{def:sectionMul}
370	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
371	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
372	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
373	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
374	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
375	  \[
376	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
377	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
378	    \;\longrightarrow\;
379	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
380	  \]
381	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
382	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
383	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
384	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
385	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
386	  the sheafification unit
387	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
388	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
389	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
390	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
391	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
392	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
393	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
394	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
395	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
396	\end{definition}
397	
398	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
399	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
400	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
401	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
402	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
403	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
404	  the two composites
405	  \[
406	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
407	    \longrightarrow
408	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
409	    \cong
410	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
411	  \]
412	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
413	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
414	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
415	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
416	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
417	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
418	\end{lemma}
419	
420	\begin{proof}
421	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
422	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
423	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
424	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
425	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
426	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
427	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
428	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
429	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
430	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
431	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
432	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
433	  section multiplications.
434	\end{proof}
435	
436	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
437	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
438	
439	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
440	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
441	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
442	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
443	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
444	
445	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
446	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
447	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
448	  \mathlibok
449	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
450	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
451	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
452	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
453	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
454	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
455	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
456	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
457	  \(1 \in A_0\).
458	\end{lemma}
459	
460	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
461	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
462	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
463	  \mathlibok
464	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
465	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
466	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
467	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
468	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
469	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
470	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
471	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
472	  extension of the graded action.
473	\end{lemma}
474	
475	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
476	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
478	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
479	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
480	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
481	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
482	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
483	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
484	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
485	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
486	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
487	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
488	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
489	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
490	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
491	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
492	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
493	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
494	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
495	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
496	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
497	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
498	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
499	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
500	  multiplication is the composite
501	  \[
502	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
503	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
504	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
505	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
506	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
507	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
508	  \]
509	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
510	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
511	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
512	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
513	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
514	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
515	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
516	\end{lemma}
517	
518	\begin{proof}
519	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
520	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
521	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
522	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
523	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
524	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
525	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
526	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
527	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
528	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
529	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
530	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
531	  commutative-semiring structure.
532	\end{proof}
533	
534	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
535	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
536	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
537	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
538	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib,
539	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
540	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
541	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
542	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).
543	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
544	  % "Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
545	  %  $$
546	  %  \Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
547	  %  =
548	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
549	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
550	  %  $$
551	  %  which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module."
552	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
553	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
554	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
555	  \(X_s\). The family
556	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
557	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
558	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
559	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
560	  action
561	  \[
562	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
563	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
564	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
565	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
566	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
567	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
568	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
569	  \]
570	  the section multiplication followed by the comparison isomorphism that
571	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using
572	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
573	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
574	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
575	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
576	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
577	\end{lemma}
578	
579	\begin{proof}
580	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
581	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
582	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
583	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
584	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
585	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
586	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
587	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
588	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
589	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
590	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
591	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
592	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
593	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
51	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
52	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
53	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
54	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
55	
56	\end{theorem}
57	
58	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
59	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
60	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
61	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
62	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
63	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
64	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
65	% there exists a finite filtration
66	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
67	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
68	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
69	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
70	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
71	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
72	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
73	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
74	\begin{proof}
75	
76	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
77	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
78	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
79	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
80	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
81	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
82	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
83	
84	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
85	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
86	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
87	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
88	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
89	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
90	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
91	  for exactly this residue.
92	
93	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
94	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
95	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
96	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
97	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
98	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
99	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
100	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
101	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
102	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
103	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
104	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
105	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
106	\end{proof}
107	
108	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
109	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
110	
111	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
112	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
113	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
114	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
115	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
116	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
117	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
118	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
119	
120	\begin{lemma}\leanok
121	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
122	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
124	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
125	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
126	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
127	\end{lemma}
128	
129	\begin{proof}
130	
131	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
132	  finitely presented, so
133	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
134	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
135	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
136	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
137	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
138	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
139	  \(f := r\).
140	\end{proof}
141	
142	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
143	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
144	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
145	  \mathlibok
146	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
147	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
148	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
149	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
150	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
151	\end{lemma}
152	
153	\begin{lemma}\leanok
154	[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
155	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
157	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
158	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
159	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
160	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
161	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
162	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
163	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
164	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
165	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
166	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
167	  induction).}
168	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
169	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
170	  \(A_f\).
171	\end{lemma}
172	
173	\begin{proof}
174	
175	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
176	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
177	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
178	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
179	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
180	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
181	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
182	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
183	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
184	\end{proof}
185	
186	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
187	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
188	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
189	  \mathlibok
190	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
191	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
192	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
193	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
194	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
195	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
196	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
197	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
198	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
199	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
200	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
201	\end{lemma}
202	
203	\begin{lemma}\leanok
204	[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
205	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
207	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
208	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
209	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
210	  exact sequence
211	  \[
212	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
213	  \]
214	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
215	\end{lemma}
216	
217	\begin{proof}
218	
219	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
220	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
221	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence.
222	\end{proof}
223	
224	\begin{lemma}\leanok
225	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
226	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
228	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
229	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
230	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{proof}
234	
235	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
236	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
237	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
238	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
239	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
240	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
241	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
242	\end{proof}
243	
244	\begin{lemma}\leanok
245	[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
246	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
248	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
249	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
250	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
251	\end{lemma}
252	
253	\begin{proof}
254	
255	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
256	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
257	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
258	\end{proof}
259	
260	\begin{lemma}\leanok
261	[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
262	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
264	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
265	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
266	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
267	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
268	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
269	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
270	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
271	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
272	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
273	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
274	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
275	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
276	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
277	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
278	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
279	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
280	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
281	\end{lemma}
282	
283	\begin{proof}
284	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
285	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
286	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
287	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
288	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
289	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
290	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
291	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
292	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
293	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
294	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
295	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
296	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
297	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
298	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
299	  free over \(A_f\).
300	\end{proof}
